figures.caption: Figure 1: Energy-Based Information Preservation. ERSM adaptively retains semantically relevant features while suppressing background noise.

Figure 4: Robustness to Feature Removal on ResNet-50 Res 256 Patch 1. Comparison of classification accuracy under progressive patch deletion using Energy-based ranking (red) versus Random selection (blue).
Figure 5: ERSM Improvements. Left: The original image with baseline prediction (Wrong). Right: The ERSM prediction (Correct), showing the learned mask fading out the misleading background.
Figure 6: Failure Mode. (a) The model correctly finds the bird but misclassifies it (fine-grained error). (b) The model fails to mask the background, leading to confusion.

Figure 2: Overview of the Energy-Regularized Spatial Masking (ERSM) framework. (a) A frozen feature extractor encodes the input image. (b–c) The feature map is partitioned into latent tokens. (d) Unary and 8-neighbor pairwise interactions define a token-wise energy. (e) The resulting spatial energy map suppresses redundant background regions. (f) The refined features are passed to the prediction head for classification.

Figure 3: Accuracy under progressive patch removal on the experimental CNN. ERSM preserves more accuracy than random deletion, peaking after removing 20%–25% of patches, highlighting its implicit featureselection ability.

Table 2: Comparison of Baseline (frozen backbone) against ERSM with different Patch Sizes (P) and Input Resolutions (Res). The – symbol denotes cases where the patch size does not divide the feature map. Baseline results are grouped as they only depend on resolution. Peak results are reported.
